Title: The Daily Laws by Robert Greene

January
Life Task: identify the work that feels natural and commits you for decades. Greene uses Napoleon and Da Vinci as examples of focused long-term orientation.

February
Apprenticeship: submit to training, accept boredom, and gather practical feedback loops from mentors.

March
Social intelligence: observe group incentives before speaking. Read the room before presenting ideas.

April
Never outshine the master: protect relationships with authority while still delivering high-value outcomes.

May
The coquette strategy: create strategic distance to preserve interest and avoid predictable behavior.

June
Authenticity under pressure: withstand criticism by anchoring to your life task and process.

July
Study rivals without obsession: borrow techniques and keep your own voice intact.

August
Creative timing: release work when attention cycles favor your message and your allies are ready.

September
Power with restraint: avoid tactical hell by choosing fewer, better battles.

December
Cosmic sublime: zoom out, accept scale, and maintain perspective that outlasts ego conflicts.
